anchor.snippet: The problem was to devise a walk through the city that would cross each of those bridges once and only once.
Euler proved that the problem has no solution.

annotations.label: Graph from land masses and bridges

anchor.snippet: one replaces each land mass with an abstract

annotations.label: Entering equals leaving at non-terminal vertex

anchor.snippet: during any walk in the graph, the number of times one enters a non-terminal vertex equals the number of times one leaves it

annotations.label: Even-degree condition for interior land masses

anchor.snippet: the number of bridges touching that land mass must be even
The degree of a node is the number of edges touching it.
a necessary condition for the walk of the desired form is that the graph be connected and have exactly zero or two nodes of odd degree
Such a walk is now called an Eulerian trail or Euler walk in his honor.

annotations.label: Endpoints of an Eulerian path with odd-degree nodes

anchor.snippet: if there are nodes of odd degree, then any Eulerian path will start at one of them and end at the other

annotations.label: Königsberg graph has no Eulerian path

anchor.snippet: Since the graph corresponding to historical Königsberg has four nodes of odd degree, it cannot have an Eulerian path.
Such a walk is called an Eulerian circuit or an Euler tour
Such a circuit exists if, and only if, the graph is connected and all nodes have even degree.
All Eulerian circuits are also Eulerian paths, but not all Eulerian paths are Eulerian circuits.

annotations.label: Present-day five-bridge configuration admits Eulerian path

anchor.snippet: an Eulerian path is now possible, but it must begin on one island and end on the other
the configuration of the 45 major bridges in Bristol is such that an Eulerian circuit exists
